page: Vultr voc-s-8c-64gb-1280s-amd is an Optimized Cloud Compute server configured with 8 vCPUs and 64.0 GB of system memory, yielding a 1:8 vCPU-to-RAM ratio. The instance is powered by an AMD EPYC-Rome processor operating at 2.0 GHz with x86_64 architecture, featuring 8 physical cores and a shared CPU allocation. Local storage is provided via a bundled 1280 GB NVMe SSD, and the configuration includes one complimentary public IPv4 address.

Benchmark data indicates strong single-core CPU performance, with stress-ng single-core tests placing in the top 25% at 2933.77. However, multi-core CPU performance falls into the average tier, with a stress-ng multi-core score of 23239.79 and a PassMark CPU score of 18130.87. Memory performance is a notable strength, with the PassMark memory score ranking in the top 25% at 2766.09, supported by a read-only cached small-block memory bandwidth of 1026.89 GB/sec. Database operations and Redis SET operations perform at average levels, yielding 3935.1 operations and 15.7 million operations per second respectively.

The primary tradeoff of this instance is its shared CPU allocation, which may introduce performance variability under sustained multi-core loads despite the strong single-core benchmark results. From a cost-efficiency perspective, the high memory density of 8.0 GB per core and the inclusion of a large 1280 GB NVMe SSD provide a resource-dense profile for memory-intensive workloads without the premium of dedicated CPU instances. This server is configured for memory-optimized tasks, caching layers, database hosting, and static web serving.
description: Vultr voc-s-8c-64gb-1280s-amd is an Optimized Cloud Compute server featuring 8 vCPUs and 64.0 GB of RAM, powered by an AMD EPYC-Rome processor running at 2.0 GHz on an x86_64 architecture. It includes a bundled 1280 GB NVMe SSD and one public IPv4 address. Operating with a shared CPU allocation, the server exhibits strong single-core CPU and memory performance, with stress-ng single-core and PassMark memory benchmarks both ranking in the top 25%. Conversely, multi-core CPU metrics and database operations fall into the average tier. The 1:8 vCPU-to-RAM ratio makes this configuration highly resource-dense, offering qualitative cost efficiency for memory-heavy workloads. However, the shared CPU allocation presents a tradeoff for sustained multi-threaded processing. This instance is suitable for memory-intensive applications, caching systems, database hosting, and static web serving.
